description:
You are helping a data-science colleague clean a small legacy dataset that arrived in the wrong text
    encoding.  
    The working directory tree already contains a single file:

        /home/user/data/raw/customers_win1252.csv          (encoded in Windows-1252)

    Goal  
    ----  
    1. Convert the file to UTF-8 and store the result at:

           /home/user/data/clean/customers_utf8.csv

       • The **byte-level** encoding of the new file must be UTF-8.  
       • Every character that appears in the source file must survive unchanged, only the
         byte-sequence must differ (i.e. no � replacement characters).  
       • Line endings must stay LF (Unix style).  
       • Preserve the exact same column order and delimiter (the file is comma-separated).  

    2. Create a human-readable conversion report at:

           /home/user/data/clean/conversion.log

       The log must be plain text (UTF-8) with **exactly** the following five labelled lines and no extra
       whitespace or blank lines:

           File: customers_win1252.csv
           SourceEncoding: Windows-1252
           TargetEncoding: UTF-8
           RowsProcessed: 4
           Status: SUCCESS

       Notes on the required format:  
       • Capitalisation and spelling must match precisely.  
       • Each label is followed by a single space and then its value.  
       • End the last line with a terminating newline character (LF).

    3. After finishing, you should be able to run:

           file /home/user/data/clean/customers_utf8.csv

       and see a description that mentions “UTF-8”.  
       (The automated tests will check this via the file’s actual byte encoding, not the
       `file` command output.)

    Constraints & hints  
    -------------------  
    • You do **not** have root privileges; stay completely inside /home/user.  
    • Feel free to create the directory /home/user/data/clean if it does not exist.  
    • Any tool available in a standard Debian/Ubuntu environment (iconv, recode, Python, etc.) is fine,
      but the final files **must** match the specifications above exactly.  
    • You will probably only need three or four commands in total: one to make the target directory,
      one to perform the conversion, another to count rows, and one to write the log.

    Verification  
    ------------  
    The automated tests will confirm that:

      1. /home/user/data/clean/customers_utf8.csv exists and is encoded in UTF-8.  
      2. Its textual contents (after decoding) are byte-for-byte identical to the source file’s
         textual contents.  
      3. /home/user/data/clean/conversion.log exists, is UTF-8, and matches the five-line
         format shown above *exactly*.  
      4. The value after “RowsProcessed:” is the correct number of data rows *including*
         the header line.

    Proceed in the terminal to accomplish the task.
truth:
Initial state
    -------------  
    1. Directory /home/user/data/raw exists, permissions 755.  
    2. File /home/user/data/raw/customers_win1252.csv exists, permissions 644,
       encoded in Windows-1252 with LF line endings and the following *decoded* text
       (4 lines, including header):

           id,name,city
           1,José,Montréal
           2,Léa,Berlin
           3,O'Connor,Dublin

       When encoded in Windows-1252, the bytes for “José” are 4A 6F 73 E9 and for “Montréal” are
       4D 6F 6E 74 72 E9 61 6C.

    Expected final state
    --------------------  
    1. Directory /home/user/data/clean exists, permissions 755.  
    2. /home/user/data/clean/customers_utf8.csv exists, permissions 644,
       encoded in UTF-8, LF endings, and its UTF-8 decoded text is **identical** to that shown
       above under item 2 (header plus three data rows).  
    3. /home/user/data/clean/conversion.log exists, permissions 644, encoded in UTF-8 and
       containing exactly:

           File: customers_win1252.csv\n
           SourceEncoding: Windows-1252\n
           TargetEncoding: UTF-8\n
           RowsProcessed: 4\n
           Status: SUCCESS\n

       (The “\n” indicates a single LF byte 0x0A.)

    The test harness will:

      • Compare byte-level content of the two CSV files after converting the source to UTF-8 in memory.  
      • Read conversion.log and assert an exact five-line match with the text and newline placement
        given above.  
      • Verify that customers_utf8.csv is encoded as UTF-8 (by checking that it has no bytes above
        0x7F that are illegal in UTF-8 and that the header characters are standard ASCII).
